name: Caro-Kann Defence
description: Black meets 1.e4 with c6: Advance, Exchange, Classical, Fantasy, Panov and sidelines.
source: curated repertoire (scripts/repertoires/caro-kann.pgn) + lichess explorer, ratings 1400/1600/1800, speeds blitz/rapid

children.comment: Keep the pawn chain intact; the c-file opens and Black is at least equal there.
The ideal square: the knight eyes d4 and can never be kicked by a pawn.
Tuck the bishop away before White gains time on it with Nh4 or Nd2-f1-g3.
Flexible: the knight supports ...c5 and keeps e7 free for the other knight.
The trade helps Black: the e2/d3 bishop was White's best piece. Continue ...Ne7, ...c5.
Fix the kingside; the g6 bishop is safe and g4 is weak.
Hit the centre while White chases air on the kingside.
Ambitious but loosening.
Always answer h4 with ...h5 in these structures: it stops g4-g5 and gives the bishop the g6-h7 retreat for good.
White's pawns look scary but every one of them is a weakness; develop with ...e6, ...c5 and ...Nc6.
The whole point of the Caro-Kann: this bishop gets out before ...e6 locks it in. Compare the French, where it stays buried on c8.
Full pressure on the c-file; ...Bb5 comes to trade White's good bishop.
Target b2: White's queenside is stuck defending.
The standard trick: block the b-file with gain of time on the queen.
Hitting b7 and d5.
Develop with tempo: the bishop provokes weaknesses before ...e6 closes the diagonal.
Keep the pin; if g4 then ...Bg6 and White's king is airy.
Pin the knight before White plays h3; ...e6 comes next.
The bishop has done its job on b4; from e7 it covers the kingside while Black plays ...Bf6 or ...Nf4 against d4.
Fianchetto and blockade d4; the bishop on b7 is excellent.
The Nimzo treatment: pin, then take on c3 or d5 at the right moment.
Easy development; the d5 pawn is safe and White's d4 is just as isolated.
Solid: blockade the d-pawn with pieces, castle fast.
Hit the queen, then castle. Black's position has no targets.
The tabiya. Black castles short next; the h5 pawn is more weak than scary in the endgame.
Covers e5 and prepares ...Ngf6 without allowing doubled pawns tricks.
Give the bishop the h7 hole before h5 comes.
Comfortable: trade the bishops, castle, play ...c5.
Again the bishop comes out first. White's only try for advantage is chasing it, which costs time.
Transposes to the Classical: everything below applies.
Heading for c6 or g6; ...O-O and ...Nd7 follow.
Pile on d4 via the pin.
The point of the whole setup: White's centre dissolves and the doubled c-pawns remain.
The thematic strike: the pinned c3 knight cannot help d4.
Pin the knight: e4 is under real pressure now.
A French where White spent a move on f3 and then played f4 anyway: a full tempo down.
The calm answer: develop, castle, and hit the centre when White's king is still in the middle.
Develop carefully, return the pawn for an endgame if needed; ...Ngf6 and ...Qd8 untangle.
A clean extra pawn: this is why 6.d4 does not work.
Give up the bishop, take the centre: White's doubled f-file dreams need the d4 break, and it always costs a pawn or the centre.
The most annoying reply: White cannot build the big centre with d4 in comfort.
A harmless Exchange: the early Nc3 blocks the c3 pawn White wants in that structure.
A Panov where Black never had a worse moment.
Recover the pawn with a tempo on d5; 4...Qxd5 5.Nc3 develops White for free.
Claim the full centre: against the King's Indian Attack, Black simply takes the space White declined.

variations.name: Advance system
variations.intro: White grabs space with 3.e5. Black develops the light-squared bishop OUTSIDE the pawn chain first, then builds the solid e6/c6 wall and strikes at the centre later.

variations.name: Exchange
variations.intro: 3.exd5 looks harmless but White wants the c3/Bd3/Bf4 grip. Black equalises with quick piece play against b2 and the c-file.

variations.name: Classical
variations.intro: 3.Nc3 is the classical main line. Black gives up the centre pawn for rock-solid development: ...Bf5, ...e6, ...Nd7 and a position with no weaknesses.

variations.name: Fantasy
variations.intro: 3.f3 protects e4 and dreams of a big centre. Answer in French style with ...e6: f3 does nothing useful there, and Black strikes back with ...e5.

variations.name: Panov Attack
variations.intro: 3.exd5 cxd5 4.c4 turns the game into an isolated-queen-pawn battle. Black develops against d4/d5, trades pieces, and enjoys the better endgame.

variations.name: Two Knights and sidelines
variations.intro: Everything without 2.d4: the Two Knights (2.Nc3 + 3.Nf3), the accelerated Panov 2.c4, and the King's Indian Attack setup 2.d3.
